entries.text: ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course

This material is for the personal use of a delegate attending a course presented by BSI.No Part of the materials may be reproduced, stored electronically, or transmitted in any form or by any means without the prior written consent of BSI.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Be aware of the emergency exits
Please switch mobile phone to silent
Please do not use recording devices

Please observe the following key points:For your personal safety, please be aware of the emergency exits from your classroom and the building.The tutor will inform you of the nearest restrooms.Please do not leave valuable items unattended in the classroom. Keep them with you or make other arrangements for their safekeeping.Please be considerate of other delegates, and avoid distractions from the beeping/flashing of your mobile phone.Please do not use recording devices since they may restrict free discussion.The tutor will inform you of the lunch and break schedule. Please return to class on time.The tutor will inform delegates of any area(s) known to be available for smoking.If there are any special needs, please confirm these now.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Materials:SlidesReference materialsLoan copy of ISO 19650-3:2020

This course consists of tutorials, delegate activities, and discussions.Your Delegate Workbook contains all materials related to this course, including slides, activities and reference materials. Please only refer to the answers for each activity after completing it, or if you’re really stuck.You will get the most out of this course by being an active Participant, asking questions, and engaging in discussions and activities. Remember there are no silly questions and please feel free to seek the help of your tutor as needed.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Background and context for managing asset information
Supply-side perspective (lead appointed parties and their delivery teams)
Asset information model and combining operational and delivery phase information management

This course is structured into four sessions:Background and context: An overview of why it’s important to manage asset information and how ISO 19650-3 fits into the overall ISO 19650 seriesThe asset owner/operator responsibilities concerning collaborative BIM: This session explains about the overall information management process, the need to set information requirements, and other preparatory tasks before appointing a contractor or works teamThe supply-side (contractor or works team) responsibilities concerning collaborative BIM: Developing a plan for how information is going to be produced and delivered, mobilizing the team, producing information, and submitting the information model to the asset owner/operatorThe Asset Information Model (AIM), plus bringing together ISO 19650 Parts 2 and 3: This latter topic is about understanding how operational and delivery phases of the lifecycle interact with each other and what this means, in terms of the standards and implementing collaborative BIMThe course finishes with an action planning session, to help you start to plan your next steps in putting ISO 19650-3 into practice in your organization.Two short breaks will be taken at suitable times in the morning and afternoon. 45 minutes will be given for a lunch break. Additional breaks may be taken as long as agreed by delegates and tutor, and all learning objectives are met. Course activities will be at the tutor’s discretion, depending on time and delegate needs. Finish times may therefore differ to those advertised.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course

Your tutor(s) will introduce themselves.Your turn: Delegate nameOrganization and product or serviceJob position or roleAny specific questions/problems to be answered/expectations from the courseSomething interesting about youOne specific reason for attending today
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course

This course gives building/infrastructure asset owners/operators, information managers, and asset contractors a detailed understanding of the ISO 19650-3 standard.The course explains the different responsibilities of each organization involved in specifying, providing, and managing information for the operational phase of an asset.

The intention of this course is to help delegates implement ISO 19650-3 on their built assets, as part of a wider implementation of collaborative BIM across the whole asset lifecycle.This course will deliberately not discuss other aspects of BIM according to ISO 19650, including the other standards involved, except where they directly relate to ISO 19650-3.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Upon completion of this course, you will be able to:
Describe how key ISO 19650 Asset Information Management (AIM) processes are applied during the operational phase of an asset’s lifecycle
Identify the key elements needed to formulate information requirements for the operational phase of an asset
Recognize how asset information (ISO 19650-3) and project delivery information (ISO 19650-2) can be managed in parallel
Explain how asset information is delivered and reviewed through Common Data Environment (CDE) processes
Learning objectives describe in outline what you will know by the end of this course.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Background and context for managing asset information

Not only are buildings and infrastructure assets becoming more technically complex, with more sophisticated structural design and more sophisticated environmental systems, but they are now being asked to perform better. This move to improve performance is both through tighter regulation, such as lower energy consumption, and through financial pressure, where asset owners can afford less downtime and need better operational delivery to their businesses. On top of all this, is our generally increasing expectations of our lifestyles – faster, more reliable transport, greater demands for energy and communication and greater demand coming from more people as economies develop.All the above factors mean that having reliable and accessible information about each asset is becoming more and more important. Information is needed so that assets can be managed better and so that resources can be used more efficiently.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Three key enablers of good asset management:
Information to support knowledge and decision-making

It is worth taking a step back from asset information, to remind ourselves what asset management itself is all about. For any organization that owns assets to support its business or operations, these need to be managed so that the organization can comply with some fundamental requirements:Knowing what assets it actually owns, or operatesComplying with legal requirements (health and safety, environmental etc.)Understanding its operational costs, Particularly with increasing pressure on budgetsKnowing the condition of its assets and where the weak points in its asset base might beBeing able to look after and maintain its assets as efficiently as possible. This is another aspect of the budget problemAsset management is governed by a series of International Standards, for example, the ISO 55000 series. ISO 55001, in Particular, sets out the requirements for a management system for asset management. The ISO 55000 standards identify 3 key enablers of good asset management:An organizational structure that acknowledges asset management and supports itAccess to information about the assets that can be used by the workforce and the management of the organization to support decision-making and day-to-day asset managementA workforce with the knowledge and skill to manage assets successfullyPart 3 is the focus of this course, and ISO 19650-3 when considered in the context of collaborative BIM.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
The definition of ‘asset’ and its scale
“Item, thing or entity that has the potential or actual value to an organization” - ISO 19650-1, based on ISO 55000
Scale of an asset is completely open to interpretation.

It is worth reminding ourselves what the formal definition of an asset is. This slide reproduces the definition from ISO 19650-1, which is based on the definition from ISO 55000.There are no pre-judgements for how big or small an asset in the built environment can be. The definition applies to physical built assets of any size. This ranges from whole estates or campuses or infrastructure networks, down through individual buildings or pieces of infrastructure, through Parts of buildings (e.g. rooms) and to items of equipment or plant.An organization can take an entirely flexible approach to defining its assets. For example, a university may class its entire campus as an asset and, within that, consider each building, road, car park, energy network etc. to be a separate asset. Within the buildings, key rooms may be separate assets (e.g. lecture theatres, laboratories) and key items of plant may also be assets (e.g. the boiler plant in the energy centre).The main consideration is that assets should be identified at whatever scale and in whatever hierarchy is most sensible for them to be managed as effectively as possible.Assets in the built environment do not have to be fixed, they may be movable or temporary structures.The value of an asset is not just financial, though that is the most common way of valuing a built asset. The value may be in what other processes it enables, for example, an air-conditioning system enables a building to be used during hot weather. Or the value may be in the reaction it creates, for example, the lobby of a 5* hotel makes guests feel that they are in a high-quality establishment (and therefore supports the prices charged for rooms and meals).The value of an asset is not static and usually the value of the physical asset declines during its lifetime due to its aging and other assets being created that are newer and have better functionality. But asset value can also increase, this is Particularly the case with developed land (and by extension, the value of the building on the land can also increase).
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Centre for Economic and Business ResearchArcadis Global Built Asset Wealth Index Report 2015

This chart shows the built asset wealth from the top 32 countries in the world. These countries represent 87% of global GDP. Figures are quoted in trillions of US dollars, corrected for purchasing power parity ($1 trillion = $1 000 billion = $1 000 000 million). China and USA are at the top of the list with Japan and India at numbers 3 and 4. Then a series of European, Asian, and American countries. The UK is at 13, with a built asset wealth of just under $5 trillion. Compare this with the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) of the UK, which was $2.6 trillion in 2016. So, asset wealth is about two years’ worth of all economic activity in the UK.Although these figures are several years old, they still give an indication of the order of magnitude of built asset value in different countries. These assets exist to support commercial activities and public services and deliver value to humanity.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Improving the quality of asset information
Asset information is frequently incomplete, inaccurate, ambiguous, or inaccessible

Even though assets are important to all types of organizations, the information that accompanies them is often not well managed or maintained. The shortcomings in asset information mirror those that have been observed in project information through a variety of research projects:Information might be incomplete, not giving all the information that is needed to manage the asset or its partsInformation might be inaccurate, not representing the current state or condition of the assetInformation might be ambiguous, being present in two or more different places and not agreeingIn addition, asset information might be inaccessible. An example of this is where it has been provided as the output from a project as a handover of operation and maintenance manuals or similar documents. These might be provided on a range of media (paper, CD, electronic files) and completely unstructured so that although the information exists, the recipient cannot access it in practical terms. Also, the media might become obsolete so that it cannot be accessed.One of the major challenges with asset information is that it comes from such a wide range of sources during the asset’s lifetime, and it has to be managed and maintained over a timescale that runs to decades. This is a problem that usually does not arise on a design and construction project where the timescale is a few years.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Reasons for adopting Collaborative BIM in asset management
Enhances asset information quality and usefulness
Supports better business and operational outcomes
Reduces asset -related costs for public services, freeing resources
Lowers operating costs for private sector owners/ operators
Minimizes downtime and improves critical asset identification

The benefits of adopting collaborative BIM in asset and facilities management are to improve the quality and usefulness of asset information. Therefore, it can enable better outcomes in the overall business or operation of the asset owner/operator. For example:Public sector service providers can reduce their asset-related costs to free up resources for front-line services, and improve efficiency in the use of assetsPrivate sector asset owners and operators can reduce their operating costs, either so that they can become more competitive in the marketplace, or so that they can increase profits for shareholders, or increase spending in other Parts of their business (e.g. Research and development (R&D)) Other benefits would include less downtime, and the better identification of business-critical assets and systemsIt has been estimated that over the lifetime of an asset (typically 40-60 years for a building and 80-120 years for a piece of infrastructure), the savings in lifecycle costs could amount to 30%. The source of these cost savings is in more efficient management of the asset as a whole and the components within it, for example:Accurate handover information means that initial surveys to compile asset registers are not requiredAccurate component/system information means that maintenance and repair tasks are more efficient - the exact location of the item is known, the precise spares/replacement Parts/tools to take to the job are knownAccurate information on component condition means that its end-of-life can be predicted more precisely, and there is less likelihood of replacing the component unnecessarily early or having the component fail in serviceOn top of the financial savings, there are benefits in an organization having better managed assets because it has better quality information. This could translate into higher asset utilization, better customer satisfaction, and better understanding about which of its assets are most business-critical.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Activity 1: Why might your organization want to manage asset information?
Discuss what factors would be most important to your organization in managing asset information.
If your organization does not own or operate built assets, think about one of your clients instead.
What factors would be most important to your organization (or to one of your asset-owning clients) in managing asset information?

Activity 1: Why might your organization want to manage asset information?Purpose: To recognize why asset information is important and how it adds value to an organization that owns, occupies or uses built assets.Duration: 10 minutes activity completion10 minutes feedbackDirections: Discuss what factors would be most important to your organization (or to one of your asset-owning clients) in managing asset information. If your organization does not own or operate built assets, think about one of your clients instead.Some examples you may want to consider are:Secure storage of asset informationControlling access to asset informationProviding evidence of compliance with regulationsMinimizing the cost of managing the assetProviding support for a new business strategyMaking asset management more visible within the organizationAfter you have discussed this between you, we will have a short group discussion about everyone’s conclusions.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
What factors would be most important to your organization (or to one of your asset-owning clients) in managing asset information?
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course

ISO 19650-3 is part of the ISO 19650 series of standards. The series as a whole covers information management using BIM. ISO 19650-3 addresses the operational phase of the asset lifecycle and complements ISO 19650-2, which covers the delivery phase of assets (when they are designed and constructed).ISO 19650-4 covers the details of information exchange, including the criteria for judging quality of information deliverables.ISO 19650-5 covers the security considerations around collaborative information management in the built environment.ISO 19650-6 was published in 2025, covering the exchange of health and safety information related to the project and asset lifecycle.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Most terminology is the same as Part 2
Task and master information delivery plans
Appointing party, lead appointed parties, appointed parties

ISO 19650-3 has been designed to use many of the same terms as had already been used in Part 2. For those who are already familiar with ISO 19650-2, this is intended to make it easier to understand Part 3. For those not familiar with Part 2, these terms are defined in ISO 19650-1 and ISO 19650-2.The organizations involved in information management are illustrated in Figure 3 in ISO 19650-3. Please look at this now while your tutor explains the relationships represented in the figure.The appointing party is the principal specifier of information and receiver of information. In most cases, they will be the asset owner or operator. Lead appointed parties are selected by the appointing party and instructed to deliver information relating to the technical work they are carrying out in relation to the asset. Lead appointed parties are primarily managing the information processes, the actual information is produced by the appointed parties. But in many cases, a lead appointed party and an appointed party will be the same organization, so the delivery team will have a very simple structure.Where the hierarchy is more complicated, multiple task teams will be identified within the delivery team - Each responsible for one aspect of information production. In most cases, the task teams will be aligned with the appointed parties (one appointed party per task team) but it is possible for a task team to involve several appointed parties and for one appointed party to represent several task teams.The other terms on this slide will be introduced at various points during the course, so will not be explained in detail here.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Some terminology is adapted from Part 2

A number of the terms used in ISO 19650-3 have similar terms in Part 2. Each of the terms on this slide has a ‘project’ equivalent in ISO 19650-2. You might already be familiar with these, and the meaning between the two standards is essentially the same.These terms are introduced later in the course.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Enterprise systems (Clause 5.1.10) used elsewhere in the appointing party (asset owner/operator) that can hold information produced following a trigger event
Trigger event (defined in ISO 19650-1, Clause 3.2.13): “Planned or unplanned event that changes an asset or its status during its lifecycle which results in information exchange”

There are a few new terms used in ISO 19650-3 that are not used in ISO 19650-2.Trigger events are the things that happen during the operational life of an asset that create the opportunity for new information to be produced (such as a component being replaced at the end of its life, with information about the new component being needed). Trigger events can arise for all sorts of reasons and will be discussed in more detail in session 2.Enterprise systems are the existing software tools used by the appointing party (asset owner/operator) to run and manage its business operations. Where appropriate, these existing systems can be used to store some of the asset information that the asset owner/operator wants to use in its asset management activities.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course

This section looks at asset information management from the point of view of the asset owner/operator.In terms of ISO 19650-3, the asset owner/operator is called the appointing party. They are in overall control of the information management process.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Trigger events in asset information management
Examples of trigger events are given in ISO 19650-3, Annex A, Clause A.3

The principal difference between identifying information requirements for a project and for an in-use asset is that a project follows a typical sequence of events and activities which is, at high-level, pretty much the same from project to project. This is often called the project plan of work, or the BIM Execution Plan (BEP), and different countries have their own versions. Asset operation, on the other hand, does not follow such a universal pattern.The asset lifecycle is a combination of planned and unplanned events, which results in some work being done to the asset and, therefore, some new asset information becoming available. It is not even possible to specify a broad sequence or order for these activities, since the planned activities will depend entirely on the nature of the asset and its components; and unplanned events, by their very nature, can occur at any time and at any frequency.In terms of ISO 19650-3, these events are called ‘trigger events’ as each one triggers the information management process.Examples of planned events:Planned maintenanceRefurbishment/redecorationChanges of contractor/facilities manager/asset managerEnd of lifeExamples of unplanned events:Creating a new capital project and receiving the handover informationComponent breakdownsWeather or natural events (e.g. storms, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions)Changes to external factors (e.g. new regulations, changes to the commercial or political environment)
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Step 1Getting ready to manage asset information

Please refer to Figure 4 in the loan copy of ISO 19650-3. The slide shows a simplified representation of this figure but it is important to study the detail as well as this bigger picture.The information management process in ISO 19650-3 has eight steps, and each of these is broken down into more detail in the standard. Each step corresponds to a sub-clause within Clause 5 of ISO 19650-3.We can see from the slide and from Figure 4 that the process splits into three pathways, depending on the circumstances in which information is being received by the appointing party (asset owner/operator). The upper and lower pathways are followed for planned trigger events and unplanned trigger events respectively. The middle pathway is followed when an existing asset is acquired from its previous owner. Before these pathways, there is a set of preparatory activities; during which the appointing party is setting up the management environment. After the pathways, there is a close out to the Particular trigger event that has occurred and then there are some repeat loops that keep the information management process running for as long as is required.Looking at the detail in Figure 4, we can see that the difference between the upper and lower pathways is the positioning of the trigger event itself (the encircled pentagon symbol) in the sequence of process steps.Notes continue on next page…
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course

For planned events, the specific preparation for each trigger can and should be done in advance. This involves specifying the work to be done, tendering for an appropriate contractor (or appointing an internal team to do the work) and the appointed team getting mobilized. This means that when the trigger actually happens, the team is ready to go. This is like setting up a framework or a call-off contract.For unplanned events, the preparation cannot be done in advance. For these events, the asset owner/operator has to wait for them to happen before knowing what kind of response is going to be needed, and therefore what information deliverables will have to be specified. This also includes new-build construction works which, of course, are very well planned in their own right but in terms of the overall lifecycle of the asset (or asset portfolio), they are still the response to an unforeseen or random event. For example, construction of a new bridge to replace one that has collapsed, or construction of a new road to relieve traffic congestion, or major refurbishment of a building to accommodate changes in staff numbers or work practices.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Establish organizational information requirements, relevant assets and information requirements
Establish other supporting resources for information management

The first step in the information management process is a series of activities for the appointing party to get ready to manage asset information, in line with ISO 19650-3. Let’s look at some of the key tasks in Clause 5.1.The first task for the appointing party in Clause 5.1 is to appoint individuals to carry out the appointing party’s information management function. This is not concerned with roles and responsibilities inside the asset contractors as they are not yet known. The appointing party has to have capable people to carry out all the appointing party tasks. The scale of the team needed to do this will depend on the scale and complexity of the asset portfolio and the general importance given to asset management within the asset owner/operators organization.The next tasks are to work out and document the organizational and asset information requirements, we will say more about these on the next slides.Then the foreseeable trigger events need to be identified for advance planning for information management can be done.Notes continue on next page…
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course

In the other supporting resources, there is a long list of things to be done:If needed, the asset information model has to be defined, set up and its maintenance processes establishedWhere there are existing enterprise systems that are to be used to hold Part of the information, these need to be identified and checked to make sure they can apply the required quality checks. This may mean some customization has to be doneThe CDE workflow (process) and solution (technology) have to be documented and set up. This might involve purchasing a software product or customizing existing software systemsThe asset information standard has to be written to guide the whole asset team on how information needs to be structured and formatted. This can then be exchanged between partiesThe asset information production methods and procedures have to be written in order to guide the asset team on how information is submitted, reviewed and deemed acceptable or unacceptableAny reference information and shared resources that the asset contractors and/or works teams will need for their work has to be identifiedThe asset information protocol has to be prepared to set out the legal and contractual position regarding the information that is being delivered, including the obligations on both sides
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
ISO 19650-1: Figure 2 – Hierarchy of information requirements

Figure 2 of ISO 19650-3 is shown on-screen, it shows how the different types of information requirements relate to each other in general terms. This is an evolution of the requirements hierarchy shown in ISO 19650-1 Figure 2.In the ISO 19650-3 figure, we can see that there are four types of information requirements. Within Clause 5.1 we are only concerned with the organizational information requirements (OIR) and the asset information requirements (AIR). As we move from left to right through the figure, the information requirements become more specific and more detailed.Eventually, when we reach the EIRs, we have detailed specifications of exactly what information is needed and this forms part of the work-specification for the asset contractors and/or works teams. The result, when this work has been completed, is new information to be amalgamated into the AIM. This part of the process will be discussed on later slides.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Asset Information Model (AIM)Project Information Model (PIM)
Figure 2 – Simplified illustration of the progression of information requirements(ISO 19650-3)

Figure 2 of ISO 19650-3, shows how the different types of information requirements relate to each other in general terms. This is an evolution of the requirements hierarchy shown in ISO 19650-1 Figure 2 (see previous slide).In the ISO 19650-3 figure, we can see that there are four types of information requirements. Within Clause 5.1 we are only concerned with the Organizational Information Requirements (OIR) and the Asset Information Requirements (AIR). As we move from left to right through the figure, the information requirements become more specific and more detailed.Eventually, when we reach the EIRs, we have detailed specifications of exactly what information is needed and this forms Part of the work-specification for the asset contractors and/or works teams. The result, when this work has been completed, is new information to be amalgamated into the AIM. This Part of the process will be discussed on later slides.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Highest level expressions of need for information within the organization

Organizational information requirements:What information does the organization need to know in order to operate its business and achieve its strategy?OIRs are high level needs for information, they are not just to do with built assets:For example, an organization will have all sorts of OIRs, e.g. operating in compliance with relevant regulations, or filing their financial returns on time, or meeting customer expectationsFor the purposes of this course, we will only focus on the OIR that are concerned with built assetsThe answers that are provided to these OIRs through the information management process will help the organization in its business-related decision-making
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
OIRs are defined by the organization’s information stakeholders - internal and external
Example A: What is the operational and financial impact of asset unavailability/failure?
Example B: What are the lifecycle costs of different assets in our portfolio?
Example C: How does the actual utilization of each asset compare with our assumptions?
Example D: Are the assets in appropriate condition?

OIRs are defined by the organization’s information stakeholders. These are usually the different departments inside the organization, but may also come from outside stakeholders.What is the operational and financial impact of asset unavailability/failure? [Operations, finance]What are the lifecycle costs of different assets in our portfolio? [Asset management]How does the actual utilization of each asset compare with our assumptions? [Operations, regulator]Are the assets in appropriate condition? [Operations, marketing]The examples above show four different OIRs (actually expressed as questions but they do not have to be like this). In three of the four examples, the information is of interest to more than one stakeholder, but this need not be the case.For example A, the operations department is interested in what happens when an asset fails. This might be the power supply to a factory which might shut down all production. The operations department wants to know the impact on shifts and production levels. The finance department is also interested in this, but in terms of lost income (which may be limited if there is a buffer of stock in the warehouse).For example B, the asset management team wants to know the lifecycle costs of different assets, which may be different stores for a national supermarket. Having this estate-wide picture allows the team to prioritize its forward asset improvement programme.The examples given above are quite generic, we can imagine that many different asset owners/operators would be interested in these topics. Despite this, there is no single standard list of OIRs that apply to all organizations. There may be some standard topics but each organization will have its own drivers and priorities which will result in a bespoke set of OIRs.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
What information does the organization need to know for each asset to answer its OIRs?
Example A: Schedule of ‘what-if’ scenarios covering different types of asset failure
Example B: Annual report on operational costs this year against lifecycle cost plan for this specific asset
Example C: Quarterly report on performance of the asset against the initial assumptions for downtime of plant and machinery, plus occupancy rates of different rooms
Example D: Annual survey of critical assets

The second Part of defining information requirements is to turn OIRs into AIRs.AIRs think about the information needed at the asset level, rather than at the organizational level. In some instances, it might be difficult to differentiate between OIRs and AIRs. This is not really a problem because the ultimate objectives are to document the information that is needed, rather than worry which list it is in. They also need to provide background to the development of the more detailed EIRs.Following through the examples shown on the previous slide:Operational impact: Schedule of ‘what-if’ scenarios covering different types of asset failureLifecycle costs per asset: Annual report on operational costs this year against lifecycle cost plan for this specific assetAsset utilization: Quarterly report on performance of the asset against the initial assumptions for downtime of plant and machinery, plus occupancy rates of different roomsAsset condition: Annual survey of critical assetsAIRs are considered across a single asset (remembering that it is up to the organization to define what it means by that). If an OIR is about comparing information across multiple assets (such as examples B and C from slide 28), then we would expect each asset to have the same AIR; as this will drive the collection of the data that is needed.OIRs and AIRs are effectively private to the asset owner/operator. It is their choice whether these get issued to any appointed parties.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Identify 3-4 assets that are familiar to you on the table provided.
Put yourself in the position of the asset owner/operator, and answer the question ‘What is this asset’s purpose?’
What is the purpose of this asset?
Key information the asset owner/operator needs to know during the operational life of the asset AND trigger event connected with this
For example: Electricity substation at location XYZ
To provide domestic electricity supply to homes in ABC housing estate

Overall costs to operate the substation each year: Annual cost reportExpected year when substation will need to be replaced: Condition survey every five yearsUp-to-date list of components in the substation and their current condition/remaining life: Each maintenance/repair visit

Activity 2: Documenting high-level information requirements for assets and their associated trigger eventsPurpose: To apply the “information requirements” thought process to one or more built environment assets.Duration: 15 minutes activity completion10 minutes feedbackDirections: In small groups, as directed by your tutor, identify 3-4 assets that are familiar to you on the table provided. These might be assets owned/operated by your organizations or assets owned/operated by a client of your organization.Put yourself in the position of the asset owner/operator and think of the role of each asset in the asset owner/operator’s business. Answer the question: What is this asset’s purpose?Then, document some of the key information you would need to know about that asset and the trigger event that would initiate this. Remember, we are focusing on information that is needed for the operational phase of the asset when it is in use.After you have discussed this between you, we will have a short group discussion about everyone’s conclusions.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
What is the purpose of this asset?
Key information the asset owner/operator needs to know during the operational life of the asset AND trigger event connected with this
For example: Electricity substation at location XYZ
To provide domestic electricity supply to homes in ABC housing estate

Overall costs to operate the substation each year: Annual cost reportExpected year when substation will need to be replaced: Condition survey every five yearsUp-to-date list of components in the substation and their current condition/remaining life: Each maintenance/repair visit
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Trigger events for the asset information

As we learned earlier, trigger events are the asset lifecycle activities that affect the asset in one of the ways that the asset owner/operator is interested in. These trigger events create new or updated information that the asset owner/operator has decided they need to know about.Refer to the loan copy of ISO 19650-3 and review the list of trigger events in Annex A.3.Now think about the assets and asset information we discussed in Activity 3. Can you pick any of the trigger events out from Annex A.3 that directly relate to any of the asset information? If not, can you think of a trigger event that would be connected to the information discussed in Activity 3?
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Other preparatory tasks for the appointing party

Apart from defining the high-level information requirements (OIR and AIR) for the assets they are interested in and working out which trigger events will need to be managed, the appointing party has to set up the information management process in many other respects. The following are all activities required in Clause 5.1 of ISO 19650-3:
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Other preparatory tasks for the appointing party
Establish the asset information standard:The information standard explains how the appointing party wants information to be structured, formatted, classified, and how much detail is to be included
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Other preparatory tasks for the appointing party

Establish the asset information production methods and procedures:The production methods and procedures document any workflows for producing information, checking information, and keeping information secure that need to be followed
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Other preparatory tasks for the appointing party

Reference information and shared resources:The appointing party needs to identify any existing information that can be made available to asset contractors and any shared resources, such as report templates or databases that asset contractors could or must use. This saves time and effort, and makes deliverables consistent, so they are easier to process
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Other preparatory tasks for the appointing party
The Common Data Environment (CDE):The appointing party has to establish the CDE and make sure it can provide the functionality required by ISO 19650-3
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Other preparatory tasks for the appointing party

Links to enterprise systems:The asset information that is going to be collected and delivered to the appointing party does not all have to be stored in a single new information system. It can be stored in existing IT systems, provided these can be quality controlled in the way that ISO 19650-3 requires
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Other preparatory tasks for the appointing party

Asset information model:The AIM will need to be set up to meet the needs of the asset information (defined through the information requirements). Once set up, it is expected to persist for the entire period over which asset information is being managed. If the asset is sold to another organization, the AIM should be part of the transaction and will have value The AIM also needs to be maintained and the appointing party has to set up the processes to do this. Some maintenance might be done by themselves, other maintenance will take place when asset information is updated as and when trigger events occur
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Other preparatory tasks for the appointing party

Asset information protocol:This is a contractual document to define the rights and obligations of all the parties involved in the asset information management process. It has to be incorporated into all agreements made with asset contractors or internal works teams
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Other preparatory tasks for the appointing party
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Assemble reference information and shared resources
Establish response requirements and evaluation criteria
Decide the pathway to obtain the information
Compile tender package/request to provide service
Process step 2: Preparing to make an asset information appointment

Step 2 for the appointing party (asset owner/operator) is to invite tenders/responses from potential asset contractors. The information management process has the pathways for different procurement routes (in advance of a trigger, or in response to a trigger, or an asset acquisition).The main task is to develop the EIRs and that is what we will focus on. But the other tasks do also need to be completed.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
What specific information does the organization need to know from a Particular asset contractor to answer its AIRs?

The third Part of defining information requirements is to turn AIRs into EIRs. These are the contractual questions that you can ask your different suppliers or in-house works teams (e.g. direct labour organizations). Each asset contract will have its own set of EIRs, and these will relate to the appropriate trigger-related event (e.g. a maintenance call out).This is a very similar situation to project delivery using ISO 19650-2, if we consider each response to a trigger event to be a mini-project.So that an EIR from ISO 19650-3 does not get confused with an EIR from ISO 19650-2, the proper term used in Part 3 is “EIR (ISO 19650-3)”.Following through the examples shown earlier on slide 29, with lead appointed party indicated in square brackets:Operational impact: The dependency between each asset and each business location [facility design contract]Lifecycle costs per asset: The total cost of each maintenance or repair activity [maintenance contract]Asset utilization: The operational profile of each item of plant [building management system (BMS) contract]: The occupancy of each space/room [access control contract]Asset condition: Annual survey of critical assets [condition monitoring contract]The issue about operational impact of asset failure is that it leads to an EIR (ISO 19650-3) for whoever designed the facility to schedule out the dependencies between the asset and each Part of the business operation. The asset owner might help out by highlighting the business operations it is Particularly interested in. This information might be provided through a written schedule, or through a special 3D graphical model (click on a Part of the asset and the impacted spaces are shown), or some other means.The issue about asset utilization is that it leads to an EIR (ISO 19650-3) for the BMS contractor to configure the BMS to collect sensor data from each plant item. Plus, write a report template which pulls that data together over a user defined time-period. Another EIR (ISO 19650-3) is created for the access control system supplier so that the access system can generate daily reports on room use.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Define information to be produced/captured (including reference to level of information need)
Timing and frequency of information exchange
Acceptance criteria for each information requirement

The details of what needs to be included in an EIR are given in Clause 5.2.2 of ISO 19650-3. There are four main areas to be included:Security considerations should reflect whatever policies and procedures have been defined through ISO 19650-5The information to be captured has to be described. This could be done in a number of ways – Written lists of the information needed, cross references to external specifications (for example, if there is a national specification for an operation and maintenance manual, then this can be referred to), or links to existing forms and templates that indicate what information is to be deliveredThe timing and frequency of information exchange. For some trigger events, just one exchange at the completion of the work will be sufficient, but for others, there may need to be specific information delivered at different points during the course of the appointment, or the appointment might be for a number of years, with updated versions of the information delivered at regular intervalsFinally, the acceptance criteria need to be specified for each requirement. It is expected that these will largely be defined through the information standard, production methods and procedures, and any reference information/shared resources that have been made available by the asset owner/operator. There could also be special criteria for some information requirements, in which case these have to be explained
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Revisit the information needs and trigger events you identified in Activity 3.
Asset, OIR or AIR, trigger eventLead appointed party
For example:Substation, replacement date, condition surveySurveyor
Photos, condition assessment, remaining life (years), typical life for this type of component
Final report at end of appointment
Pdf of photos and reportCSV file of condition code and remaining life

Activity 3: Define EIRs (ISO 19650-3) and supporting information for a trigger-related appointment.Purpose:To practice turning OIRs/AIRs into EIRs for a specific asset management appointment.Duration:20 minutes activity completion10 minutes feedback and discussionDirections:As directed by your tutor, revisit the information needs and trigger events you identified in Activity 2, choose one trigger event and complete the table overleaf:Decide what type of appointed party (asset contractor or internal works team) will be appropriate to do this work. For example, if the information needed concerns the condition of that asset, then a building surveyor or civil engineering surveyor might be the right type of organization to appointNext, think about each of the components of an EIR (ISO 19650-3), as discussed on slide 37, and write some notes under each heading to define what information you (as asset owner/operator) need from your asset contractor/works team. It is OK if you decide that nothing Particular needs to be said under the security heading. As we do not have an asset information standard to refer to, under the acceptance criteria, make some notes about the format/structure/classification that has to be usedRepeat step 3 for each separate EIR that you identify in response to the trigger event. There might be just one, or there might be many separate information requirements that have to be delivered by the asset contractor – They might happen at the same time or at different timesIf you have time, go back and pick another information need/trigger to discuss and expand into a detailed EIRAfter, we will discuss some of your results in a discussion and feedback session
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Asset, OIR or AIR, trigger eventLead appointed party
For example:Substation, replacement date, condition surveySurveyor
Photos, condition assessment, remaining life (years), typical life for this type of component
Final report at end of appointment
Pdf of photos and reportCSV file of condition code and remaining life
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Supply-side perspective (lead appointed parties and their delivery teams)

This section of the course looks at the information management process from the supply-side perspective. This includes external contractors who are engaged to carry out work on the asset, and deliver updated information to the asset owner/operator. It also includes any in-house teams that are used to working on the asset, such as in-house maintenance or survey teams. Although these in-house teams are Part of the asset owner/operator’s organization, they are treated in the same way as external contractors, in that they are appointed to do a specific piece of work.As far as ISO 19650 is concerned, there does not have to be a formal contract between the parties, the requirements of information management still apply.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Allocate resources to the information managementfunction
Prepare (pre-appointment)BIM execution plan on behalf of delivery team
Assess capability and capacity of the delivery team
Prepare mobilization plan and information management risk register

There are a number of steps that the lead appointed party needs to go through. The time it takes to do this, and the complexity of the response at each step, depends on the scale and complexity of the asset information in the EIR, and of the asset management activity itself.These steps are set out in Clause 5.3 of ISO 19650-3, but the overall structure of these steps is exactly the same as defined in ISO 19650-2 for project delivery.The purpose of the response submitted back to the appointing party (asset owner/operator) is to provide evidence and assurance to the asset owner/operator that the asset contractor/in-house team knows what it is doing in relation to information management and delivery. The response is Part of the supplier proposal designed to convince the asset owner to select the lead appointed party to do the work. Other Parts of the supplier proposal will include their technical approach to the work, their price quotation for doing the work and any other added value they can bring.At this stage of the process, there might be multiple lead appointed parties competing for the appointment. This means the asset owner/operator will receive multiple responses and may have specified templates, or similar, for lead appointed parties to use to make their life easier in assessing these responses.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Considerations for the BIM execution plan (Clause 5.3.2)
The BIM Execution Plan (BEP) is just one part of the response from a lead appointed party.
Timing quantity and scope of information

The pre-appointment BIM Execution Plan (BEP) needs to consider a number of different topics. Which of these are actually included in the BEP depends on the circumstances of the appointment. For example, for some yearly maintenance being carried out as a standalone activity when there are no other asset works being done in the same area or on the same system, then the need to federate information with other delivery teams may not occur. In which case, there does not need to be any content in the BEP about this. However, there should be a record that the matter has been thought about and judged as ‘not applicable’.While some asset management appointments are extremely complicated and long term, there are also very simple and short term appointments (such as to repair or replace a single broken component). Both these extremes are catered for by ISO 19650-3.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
What does a BEP look like?

The BEP responds to the details in the EIR. There is no specified layout in the standard, however, the asset owner/operator (appointing party) might have provided a template that they require to be followed.

The arrangement of the BEP is primarily a matter for the responding organization. However, the asset owner/operator (appointing party) might have provided a template that they require to be followed.The BIM execution plan can be very simple if the scope of the appointment is straightforward and could be provided in a short document.Alternatively, if the scope of the appointment is more complicated, the BEP could include the same types of details as are indicated for a project (delivery phase) BEP:Professional resumes (CVs) of the individuals involvedObjectives and goals for collaborative production of informationOrganization charts for the delivery teamHigh level responsibility matrix, outlining the responsibilities across the delivery teamProposed amendments to the information standard (unlikely to be entertained by the appointing party, unless this is a ‘total asset management’ appointment)Proposed amendments to the information production methods and proceduresDetails of software to be used within the delivery team
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Task information delivery plans and master information delivery plan

Based on the lead appointed party responses received, the asset owner/operator selects their preferred organization.The process for completing the appointment with the selected lead appointed party (and their delivery team) closely aligns with the steps outlined in ISO 19650-2.Firstly, the pre-appointment BEP has to be confirmed, including any changes needed because of the time that has passed since the tender submission or any changes to scope introduced by the asset owner/operator.Then a detailed matrix of responsibilities for producing information within the delivery team has to be developed. Clearly, if the appointment is very simple and the team is very small, then this matrix will itself be very straightforward.There may or may not be a task team structure within the delivery team. Many simple asset management or facility management tasks might just involve one organization and a small number of site operatives, so there might not be any need for lead appointed party EIR, where the lead organization in the delivery team needs additional information from each task team in the delivery team as internal deliverables during the work.Similarly, if there is only one task team (the same organization as the lead appointed party but with the responsibility for producing the information rather than managing the process), then there is no need to collate multiple task team information delivery plans into a master information delivery plan.The appointments themselves are finalized and Clauses 5.4.6 and 5.4.7 in ISO 19650-3 list the documents to be included.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Test information production methods and procedures

Mobilizing the delivery team has three key components: To make sure that the right people are equipped to do the work (both technically and in terms of their understanding of the information management process and the requirements on them)To make sure that the IT solutions work properly (this might involve remote connections from the site and direct data feed from the site teams to the head office)To test the production methods and proceduresThis last item means checking that the processes for exchanging information work properly, that security protocols work as intended, that review procedures work, and so on.The final requirement in this step is for the lead appointed party (asset contractor or internal works team) to make sure that readiness is maintained over the duration of the appointment. This is Particularly the case if the work relates to intermittent site visits (such as yearly equipment inspections for five years or replacement of a Particular component whenever it breaks down). For a simple one-off appointment, then this requirement is unlikely to be needed.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Producing and submitting the information model
The production of information by the task team (within the delivery team)
The review of information within the delivery team (internal quality checks)
The acceptance of information by the asset owner/operator (the appointing party)

There are three key aspects to producing and submitting information in response to the exchange information requirements and in accordance with the detailed task/master information delivery plan(s) that the team has created.These are shown on the slide.We will cover these in the next few slides. As the production and submission of information is governed by the CDE workflow, we will discuss these steps in relation to the CDE concept diagram contained in ISO 19650-1.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Work in progressReview and approval at the information container level
Work in progressInformation being developed by its originator or task team, not visible to or accessible by anyone else
SharedInformation approved for sharing with other appropriate task teams and delivery teams or with the appointing party
PublishedInformation authorized for use in more detailed design, for construction or for asset management
ArchiveJournal of information transactions, providing an audit trail of information container development

Information production happens in the work in progress information state (highlighted with orange).In terms of an asset management appointment, this could mean any number of things:Recording the model/serial number details for a new piece of equipment being installed during a routine maintenance operationCompiling a survey of asset and equipment condition and confirming that equipment exists and is located, as indicated, in the asset registerPreparing an annual report on the electricity consumption across the different departments/room uses in a headquarters buildingProducing a method statement for the phased redecoration of a shopping centre while it remains in use by the general publicThis information production might only involve a single organization working on its own (a delivery team of one organization) or there might be several task teams collaborating together (the phased redecoration method statement might involve task teams responsible for general painting, floor tile replacement and installing water-saving fittings in the customer toilets).Information production also includes the task team carrying out its own checking process to make sure that it has met all of the requirements of the EIR and the master information delivery plan. Plus, to check that the information has been produced in line with the asset information standard and the asset information production methods and procedures. When information has passed these checks, it is then put in the shared state and becomes visible to other members of the delivery team.Where there is a multi-task team approach, this step does then not include the review of information across task teams.The procedures and workflows for producing information are defined in the information production methods and procedures.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Review information within the delivery team (Clauses 5.6.3-5.6.6)
SharedReview and authorization of the whole information model (possibly multiple information containers)
Work in progressInformation being developed by its originator or task team, not visible to or accessible by anyone else
SharedInformation approved for sharing with other appropriate task teams and delivery teams or with the appointing party
PublishedInformation authorized for use in more detailed design, for construction or for asset management
ArchiveJournal of information transactions, providing an audit trail of information container development

A collaborative review of information happens within the shared information state. This is where information from each task team is shared throughout the delivery team.Of course, if the delivery team is a very simple structure with just one task team in it, then this step does not require any further action than the checks that were carried out on the previous slide. However, the only difference here is that the sign-off is being given by the lead appointed party. Whereas from work in progress, the sign-off is being given by the task team/appointed party. These would be from the same organization but are unlikely to involve the same individual in both roles, unless the delivery team is extremely small (perhaps comprising of just one person) because the work item is so small.The orange highlight on the slide is deliberately shown as overlapping the review/authorize transition because the completion of this step means that the information deliverables have started the transition towards the published state (but have not completed it).The procedures for reviewing information within the delivery team will be defined in the information production methods and procedures.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Work in progressInformation being developed by its originator or task team, not visible to or accessible by anyone else
SharedInformation approved for sharing with other appropriate task teams and delivery teams or with the appointing party
PublishedInformation authorized for use in more detailed design, for construction or for asset management
ArchiveJournal of information transactions, providing an audit trail of information container development
PublishedReview and acceptance of the whole information model from the delivery team (possibly multiple information containers)
Asset acquisitiononly involves this step of information model acceptance

The final step in this process is for the information to be accepted by the asset owner or asset operator.This involves a check against the EIR and the Master Information Delivery Plan (MIDP), and that the information model contains the correct level of information (as defined in the level of information need). By extension, checking against the EIR and MIDP also involves a check against the asset information standard.The orange highlight on the slide is deliberately shown as partially overlapping the review/authorize transition because this step completes the transition towards the published state (assuming that the checks are successful).It is also important to mention the case of an asset being acquired by the appointing party. A typical example is a commercial organization buying or leasing a building for new office accommodation. In this case:The new owner has no influence over the amount and type of information that has been collated by the old ownerThe new owner can define their information requirements, as we discussed earlier, but it will not be able to impose these requirements through the property purchase and will not know how well its information requirements are met until after the transaction has been completed There might be exceptions if there are laws to keep certain types of building/property related information, but these will not cover the full range of operational information that the new owner will need.Once the new owner has reviewed the information from the old owner against its AIR and EIR, it is very likely that some surveys and investigations will be needed to collect all the missing information so that it can be put into the asset information model. This will be done through one or more follow-on appointments with delivery teams, and these activities will follow the full information management process.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Swap your groups answers from Activity 3 with another group
Acting as asset contractors, respond to the EIRs provided from the other group and write method statements for each one
EIR (ISO 19650-3) from Activity 3, lead appointed party
Information production and exchange – How created/collected, software/equipment used, format of deliverable, timing and frequency of delivery (Particularly if different from EIR)

Activity 4: Delivering asset information.Purpose:To practice responding to EIRs.Duration:20 minutes work in the same groups as Activity 310 minutes feedback and discussionDirections:As directed by your tutor, pass your group’s answers from Activity 3 to another group.For this activity, your group is one (or more) asset contractor working for whichever organization has prepared the EIRs you have been given. Your task is to respond to the EIRs and write some method statements for each one in the table overleaf. Explain:How the information would be collectedWhat format the information would be delivered in (is it documentation, 3D graphics or structured data?)What type of software you might use to capture or generate the informationWhen/how many times during your work you would deliver the information to your client (the asset owner/operator and appointing party)
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
EIR (ISO 19650-3) from Activity 3, lead appointed party
Information production and exchange – How created/collected, software/equipment used, format of deliverable, timing and frequency of delivery (Particularly if different from EIR)
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Asset information model and combining operational and delivery phase information management
This module starts to look at how asset information is stored.
BIM ISO 19650-3: Information Management in the Operational Phase of the Assets Training Course

The repository for asset information is the AIM. This is analogous to the Project Information Model (PIM) that is produced during project delivery.The AIM holds information and data about whatever is important to the asset owner/operator and is needed by them to manage the asset. Similar to the PIM, the AIM is expected to contain a mixture of:Geometrical information (3D models)Alphanumerical information (structured data) representing aspects of the asset that cannot or do not need to be captured geometricallyDocumentation (unstructured information) relating to the asset or to the objects/components within itThe AIM is intended to exist for as long as the asset exists. This means that it will always outlive any appointment that the asset owner/operator makes for asset management activities. This, in turn, means that primary ownership and accountability for the asset information model has to be with the asset owner/operator. The appointments that the asset owner/operator does make to various asset contractors/works teams (lead appointed parties) will only ever deliver additional or updated material for the AIM, not the whole AIM itself.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
The structure of the federated asset information model
Federated collection of information and data, expected to make initial implementation easier.

The AIM is a federated collection of information and data. This means that the original data does not all have to be stored in one single place or system. A range of systems might be used, including existing Computer Aided Facility Management (CAFM) systems (e.g. helpdesks, work scheduling, contract management etc.), finance systems (e.g. purchase and sales ledgers, timesheets etc.) and monitoring systems, most of which will exist in pretty much any asset owner/operator (e.g. fire alarm, energy, occupancy, security and remote condition sensing etc.) and specialist systems that may be more asset-owner specific (e.g. tenant management, asbestos schedules etc.).This is expected to make initial implementation easier since it should reuse a range of existing tools that the asset owner/operator already has. However this will have some impact on efficiency and speed of analysis since the various systems that hold Parts of the AIM will need to be connected together (through a combination of hardware, software and even human information transfer). For example:A housing association may already use an asset database to record asset detailsA facility manager (FM) contractor may already use a CAFM system to schedule and manage facilities tasks
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Maintaining the asset information model (Clauses 5.8 and 5.1.12)
Each response to a trigger event creates some new information for the AIM

Once an AIM has been created, by whatever means, it will be of very little value if it’s not maintained to keep it up to date. Rather like the physical asset itself.Indeed, the vast majority of the information transactions during the lifecycle will be about maintaining the model. Each trigger and trigger-related event creates some new information for the AIM. This starts as information produced as work in progress in the CDE workflow. When information reaches the published state, it can then be used to update the existing AIM contents. The complexity of how this information is exchanged with the asset owner will depend on the complexity of the asset management task. For simple tasks such as plant maintenance taking a few days, this might just be one exchange at the end of the task. For more complex work, such as a rolling redecoration programme, a sequence of information exchanges might be needed. In addition to these detailed considerations for maintaining the contents of the AIM, ISO 19650-3 puts a specific requirement on the asset owner/operator (appointing party) to set up a broader set of maintenance processes for the AIM. The minimum list is given in Clause 5.1.12 of ISO 19650-3.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Ideally, the AIM will always align with the state of the asset.

As we have seen, the updating of asset information on large or complex assets could be happening on an almost continual basis. Ideally, the AIM will always be aligned with the state of the asset so that the OIR and AIR can continue to be answered through running the necessary reports and analyses.This means that the systems housing the asset information need to be sophisticated enough to quarantine parts of their contents that are subject to change because of on-going asset management works. If the assets and the asset information are this complex, then the asset owner will probably conclude that it is cost-effective to invest in automated connectivity between the different federated Parts of the AIM. Plus, to provide automated information and data input from the asset contractors to reduce the amount of human handling of information to an absolute minimum.For asset owners/operators with much simpler asset information models, it may be the case that asset management activities happen on a much less intense schedule. For them, the question may be more about storing up information updates over a period of time and then processing them on a batch-basis to bring the AIM back up to date. The frequency with which this batch updating happens then needs to be considered. It may require external resources if the in-house team does not have the technical skills to update 3D models. In these cases, the automatic quarantining of data that is subject to change is unlikely to be implemented – the systems will not be that sophisticated.This becomes a balance between the costs of implementing the updates, and the risk of using out-of-date data. The asset owner/operator will need to come to a compromise about how often updates are done – This may be weekly, monthly, quarterly, or even less frequent, depending on the circumstances.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Asset in use (AIM grows during operational phase)
AIM provides reference information for the project
AIM receives relevant information from project
Information exchange(s) during and at the end of the project

This slide illustrates the situation where an existing asset provides the context for a new project (delivery phase of an asset).Time is running from left to right and the long, flat, truncated wedge at the top of the slide represents the AIM being added to, over time, during the operational phase of the asset. This activity could carry on regardless of the project (it largely depends on how the asset has been defined and what the project actually involves).The AIM at the start of the project provides a set of reference information:If the project is a refurbishment of an existing building, this could include current structural and architectural informationIf the project is a redevelopment of an empty site, this could include the location/coordinates of the site and surrounding features and any previous ground condition surveysThe point is that there will always be some pre-existing information, even if it is only the address of the site.The asset owner/operator has already developed its OIR and AIR. Perhaps the project is to build an extension to an office building on an existing site, to accommodate staff from another town who are being relocated. The relocation of staff is the trigger event, and the new project is the asset management response. For this trigger, the asset owner/operator can now develop its EIR (ISO 19650-3) – For the project.The EIR (ISO 19650-3) are passed across from the FM team to the project team. The project team then use them to help define the information required from the project delivery teams (under the project lead appointed parties). The information is delivered to the project client through the project information exchanges. The relevant information from the project then passes back to the AIM (usually at the end of the project, but this can also be during the project).
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
At the start of a project
Need to coordinate their responsibilities and roles.E.g. FM team (operational appointing party) liaises with client’s project team (delivery appointing party).
Align the asset and project information standards, production methods and procedures, protocols.E.g. Make sure naming and classification are aligned between project and asset information.
EIR (ISO 19650-3) passed across from FM to project team.Specify the information needed from the project so that FM team can manage the asset after it is handed over.

At the start of the project, there needs to be a mobilization period for the asset owner/client organization as there are a number of activities that need to be completed.Apart from making sure that the information requirements are passed across the interface between asset/facilities management and the project, the two appointing parties need to make sure that their respective roles and responsibilities are coordinated with each other. It does not matter if the project client is in-house to the asset owner (i.e. FM and project teams are different departments in the same organization) or if the project has been outsourced to a developer.The documentation that drives the information management process also needs to be in alignment. There should already be the asset information standard, asset information production methods and procedures and asset information protocol. Clearly the project-related equivalent documents need to be aligned to these asset documents, as much as is necessary. This is perhaps most important in the information standards (where exchange formats need to be compatible and ideally the same) and in the information protocols (so that there are no legal inconsistencies).This all requires a knowledgeable and assertive operational appointing party (asset management or facilities management team from within the asset owner/operator).
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
At the end of a project
By delivery appointing party against the project EIR.By operational appointing party against the EIR (ISO 19650-3)
Transfer/import the information into the AIM.Adding to/replacing information already in the AIM
