New asset might need new or amended trigger events.Reflect these in an amended information management process

When the project reaches its handover point, and the new asset is ready to be occupied or operated, then there are some close-out activities.These include making sure that the information requested in the EIR (ISO 19650-3) has been delivered and is satisfactory. Once that happens, it can be accepted. Firstly as a deliverable by the project appointing party (client) and then as a deliverable by the operational appointing party (asset/facilities management team).As far as the operational appointing party is concerned, this marks the end of the project. But there are some further activities that they need to carry out.Firstly, the information needs to be incorporated into the AIM. Depending on the nature of the project and the nature of the asset ahead of the project, this might be a combination of information being updated and entirely new information being added to the AIM.Secondly, the change that has occurred to the asset means that the whole asset information management process needs to be reviewed. There might be some new trigger events to plan for in terms of information, or some previous trigger events that are now no longer needed.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Activity 5: Implementing an asset Common Data Environment(CDE)
Discuss and identify the resources your organization or clients would need to implement or support an asset CDE.
Then, assess the feasibility of acquiring the resources provided.

Activity 5: Implementing an asset Common Data Environment(CDE).Purpose:To identify the resources required for implementing and supporting an asset CDE.Duration:20 minutes activity completion10 minutes feedback and discussionDirections:Discuss and identify the resources your organization or clients would need to implement or support an asset CDE. Consider the following categories:IT hardware and software: What level of compatibility is required?Personnel: What skill sets are necessary?Procedures: What standardized processes need to be in place?Supply chain management systems: How will data and processes be integrated?Top-level management support: What level of leadership buy-in is needed?Other factors: Are there additional considerations specific to your organization?Next, assess the feasibility of acquiring these resources:How easy or difficult would it be to put these resources in place?What resources do you already have available?Be prepared to share your findings during the feedback and discussion session.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Responsibilities of the asset owner/operator (appointing party)
Manage the links to existing enterprise systems

Managing the information requirements is a role that has a large Part to play in the early stages of asset information management. This is the development of the OIRs and the AIRs. It involves engagement with the asset stakeholders, but sets of information requirements are expected to be quite stable over time. They might alter if the organization, as a whole, changes its strategic objectives (which would give rise to new OIRs) or if some unforeseen triggers arise (which will need new AIRs to be written).Managing the CDE is very likely to be a role within the owner/operator. The exception might be if the owner/operator has outsourced its entire asset management function out to an external provider, but, in this case, the CDE will still be under the owner/operator’s ultimate control. This role is focused on the processes and procedures around the CDE.Authorizing information from suppliers is a key quality process in the overall acceptance of external data and information into the AIM. This is likely to be overseen by the information management role, even if the mechanics of running the verification and validation checks are delegated.The AIM is likely to be a more complicated data and information structure than is found on a project, principally because of the linked enterprise systems. Unless the links to these enterprise systems are automated through software/hardware, then transfers or exports/imports of data will need to be carried out manually and dedicated resource will be needed to do this.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Responsibilities of each contractor/works team (lead appointed party)
To understand the information requirements, information standards, and procedures. To follow the CDE workflow. Liaise with other delivery teams as needed.

The key responsibilities for each contractor/works team are to understand the information requirements from the owner/operator and to raise queries if the requirements are not clear.They need to arrange for the required information to be produced/collected during the course of the asset works. This could be status and condition reports, details of new equipment or Parts installed, meter readings, supporting documentation, such as warranty details, maintenance details, dates and times that work was carried out, and so on.They need to deliver their information to the appointing party using the CDE workflows and to make amendments and revisions, as needed, to get the information accepted by the asset owner/operator.There might also be the need to liaise with other lead appointed parties/delivery teams who are working at the same time on a related appointment to theirs.
ISO 19650 Part 3: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Operational Phase Training Course
Activity 6: Action planning for asset information management
Identify areas where your organization excels in asset information management with Collaborative BIM and where the biggest improvements are needed.
In your action plan, define actions, resources, and metrics needed to meet standard responsibilities and track progress toward completion.
What more do we need to do?
What resources are needed to achieve this?
How will we know it has been done?

Activity 6: Action planning for asset information management.Purpose:To reflect on the concepts and ideas discussed during this course and consider how to move forward with these in your own organization.Duration:30 minutes activity completion, or with colleagues from the same organization.Directions:Reviewing all we have covered in this course, now think about which areas your organization is most advanced in its journey towards asset information management using collaborative BIM.Think about the areas where your organization is already well advanced, and also the areas where your organization needs to make the biggest steps forward.In your action plan, describe what your organization needs to do in order to equip itself to satisfy the responsibilities allocated by the standard. Identify what resources are needed to make this happen (people, training, equipment, buy-in from directors etc.) and then indicate how you would measure that the objective has been achieved and the deadline for completion met. As this action plan is private to you, there is no need to discuss it with other delegates on the course. You probably won’t have time to finish your plan, so do as much as you can to sketch it out and then complete it later.Some ideas are included in your ‘References’ document, but use these only as a prompt for your own thoughts and ideas.
